title: Rabbit enrichment and foraging behaviour
subtitle: What rabbits need beyond food and shelter.
excerpt: A rabbit that spends most of its time at rest in a small hutch without the opportunity to forage, dig, or explore is a rabbit experiencing chronic welfare compromise. Enrichment does not require expensive equipment. It requires understanding what rabbits actually need to do and providing a space and materials to do it.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits (2019). RWAF enrichment guidance, rwaf.org.uk. RSPCA rabbit welfare campaign literature, rspca.org.uk.

glossaryTerms.definition: Objects, spaces, or management practices that allow an animal to express natural behaviours. For rabbits, this includes opportunities to dig, forage, chew, hide, explore, and social interaction. DEFRA welfare codes require that rabbits be provided with enrichment.

content.text: In the wild, a rabbit spends four to five hours a day foraging across a territory of 1 to 1.5 hectares, interspersed with periods of social grooming and rest in a burrow system. A hutch-and-run system provides the rest and the social interaction but rarely the foraging range or the ability to dig.

Scatter a portion of the daily hay or pellet ration around the run rather than placing it all in one bowl. A rabbit that has to search for food is an engaged rabbit. Stuff a willow ball or a paper bag loosely with hay; the rabbit works to pull it out. Place fresh leafy branches (apple, hazel, willow, blackcurrant) into the run for browsing and chewing.

Digging is a strong behavioural need, especially in does. A deep tray of sand or loose soil within the run satisfies this drive in a contained way. A wooden box filled with shredded paper provides a similar stimulus. Rabbits that cannot dig often channel the behaviour into digging at the run floor or corners, which is a sign of inadequate provision rather than destructive behaviour.

UK welfare codes and the RWAF both recommend a minimum of 3 metres by 2 metres as a combined hutch-and-run floor area for two medium-sized rabbits (about 5 kg each). This is the minimum; larger is better. The rabbits should be able to complete three full hops in a straight line without obstacle, stand fully upright on their hind legs, and turn around without restriction.

Rabbits are prey animals and require at least one hiding place where they can retreat without being followed. A simple cardboard box with two entrances is enough. The hiding place serves as a refuge during stress (fireworks, nearby predators, novel stimuli) and allows subdominant rabbits in a pair to retreat safely from an assertive bonding partner.
